segments.s109-g89: یکی is an indefinite subject here meaning 'a certain one / someone'. خیمه زد is a fixed verbal phrase in classical/poetic Persian meaning 'pitched a tent' (زد here is the light verb used with خیمه). بر سر او is a prepositional phrase 'over/on his head / over him' marking location; دود و قیر follows as a nominative/asydetic list giving the resulting smoke and pitch.
segments.s109-g90: سیه شد is a verbal clause 'became black/dark' (سیه = black, شد = became). جهان is the grammatical subject 'the world'. چشمها خیره is a verbless/elliptical clause common in poetry: 'eyes (were) staring/dazed'. خیر at the end is terse and ambiguous in older usage; here it functions as a comment on the outcome (literally 'goodness' but used ironically/negatively — 'no good / nothing good').
segments.s109-g91: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بگذشت (from گذشتن) here means 'passed' referring to the night (شب). The second part, روز نزدیک شد, is a simple clause 'the day became near / the day drew near' (نزدیکِ شدن expressed with adjectival نزدیک + شدن). Word order is typical: temporal clause 'چو بگذشت شب' followed by the resulting clause.
segments.s109-g92: This is a possessive/experiential construction: جهانجوی را marks the possessor/affected person with را, then چشم is the possessed item ('his sight/eye') and تاریک شد is the predicate 'became dark/dim'. In modern prose one would say 'چشم جهانجوی تاریک شد' — the poet places the possessor + را before the possessed for emphasis.
segments.s109-g93: ز لشکر ... شده is a prepositional/agentive phrase 'from the army ... had become/was taken' (ز = from/because of, لشکر = army, بهره شده lit. 'had its share' or 'a portion was taken'). تیره چشم follows as a descriptive phrase 'dark-eyed' or 'gloom fell on the eyes' — in poetry adjective phrases often follow the verbal nucleus.
segments.s109-g94: سر نامداران is 'the heads/leaders of the renowned (men)'. ازو is a prepositional/relative particle 'because of him / from him / concerning him'. پر ز خشم is an adjectival predicate 'full of anger' (پر = full (of), ز = from/because of, خشم = anger). Word order places possessor/title then the cause (ازو) then the predicate.
segments.s109-g95: از ایشان 'from/against them' marks the affected group. فراوان تبه کرد is 'did much evil / inflicted abundant harm' (فراوان = abundantly/plentifully, تبه = evil/ruin as a noun, کرد = did/caused). نیز at the end means 'also / too' and attaches to the clause.
segments.s109-g96: نبود here is negative past existence 'there was not / there had not been'. از بدبخت ماننده چیز literally 'a thing like that misfortune' (از = of/regarding, بدبخت = misfortune/unfortunate, ماننده = like/similar to, چیز = thing). Together the clause says nothing like that misfortune had occurred before.
segments.s109-g137: چو is a temporal conjunction here meaning “when.” ارژنگ is the subject (proper name). بشنید is simple past, 3rd-person singular (“he heard”). گفتار اوی is the direct object: گفتار “speech/words,” followed by the archaic/poetic possessive اوی (“his”), so the clause literally reads “when Arzhang heard his speech.” Word order (temporal marker → subject → verb → object) is typical of epic Persian.
segments.s109-g138: سوی introduces a direction (“toward”). شاه مازندران is the goal (the king of Mazandaran); in poetry the genitive relation is often compacted (شاه + place name). کرد is the past-tense verb “made/did” used in the light-verb construction کرد + روی to mean “turned (his) face.” روی here is the object “face,” so کرد روی = “turned (his) face.”
segments.s109-g139: همی رفت is a habitual/continuative past construction: همی adds the sense of continued or ongoing action and رفت is past simple “he went/proceeded.” با لشکر is the prepositional phrase “with an army.” و links the following word خواسته; in context خواسته functions adjectivally or as a participle modifying the following nouns (see next segment).
segments.s109-g140: This chunk lists the companions/booty that accompany him: اسیران “prisoners” and اسپان “horses.” آراسته is a past-participial/adjectival form meaning “adorned/arrayed” and modifies the preceding nouns (i.e. the horses — or more broadly the booty) as an attributive/predicative adjective. The whole phrase supplies the objects/companions of با لشکر above.
segments.s109-g141: سپرد is a past transitive verb “he handed/entrusted.” او is the direct object “him.” به شاه is the dative prepositional phrase “to the king.” The conjunction و links the next clause. سبک here is an adjectival/adverbial word “light” (metaphorically light of heart/easily) modifying بازگشت; بازگشت functions as the verb “returned” (past) in this poetic register, so سبک بازگشت = “he returned lightly/quickly.”
segments.s109-g142: بدان is composed of ب (to/into) + an archaic/demonstrative element meaning “that,” functioning as “to that.” برز کوه is a poetic compound meaning “lofty/high mountain” (برز “high/exalted” before کوه). آمد is the main verb “came.” از پهن دشت is a prepositional phrase “from the broad plain,” where از marks origin and پهن is the adjective modifying دشت. The clause thus gives the movement: he came to that high mountain from the wide plain.
segments.s109-g33: This relative clause begins with the universal demonstrative phrase 'هر آنکس که' = 'whomever/any person who'. Word order is subject-relative: 'هر آنکس که' + verb 'بینی' (you see). The preposition-like 'ز' here links the following pair 'پیر و جوان' as the two possibilities being described ('of old and young').
segments.s109-g34: Imperative clause: 'تنی کن' is an order with 'کن' as the imperative ‘do/make’ and 'تنی' (literally 'bodily') meaning 'make him a corpse / deal him a mortal blow' in this context. The following 'که' introduces the result clause ('so that') and 'نباشد روان' is a negative-subjunctive/result phrase meaning 'his soul is not (with him)' — i.e. his spirit departs.
segments.s109-g35: 'وزو' is an archaic/contractional particle here (literally 'و ز او' = 'and of it/from it') introducing what follows. 'هرچ آباد بینی' = 'whatever inhabited/settled place you see' (هرچ = 'whatever', آباد = 'inhabited/settlement', بینی = 'you see'). 'بسوز' is an imperative: 'burn (it)!'.
segments.s109-g36: 'شب آور' is another imperative phrase: 'bring/bring about night' (آور = 'bring'). The prepositional phrase 'به جایی که باشی به‌روز' contains a relative clause introduced by 'که' = 'where', with 'باشی' in the subjunctive/present referring to 'you will be/are to be' there, and 'به‌روز' = 'by day / in daylight' qualifying the place/time.
segments.s109-g37: 'چنین' = 'thus/so' introduces the instruction; 'تا' = 'until' introduces the temporal limit. 'به دیوان رسد آگهی' = 'news/information reaches the دیوان'. Here 'رسد' is third-person singular (present/subjunctive/optative sense used poetically) meaning 'arrives/reaches', and 'آگهی' = 'news/information'.
segments.s109-g38: Imperative construction: 'جهان کن' = 'make the world' (کن imperative), followed by 'سراسر' = 'entire/throughout' and the prepositional phrase 'ز دیوان تهی' = 'empty of the دیوان' (ز ... تهی = 'devoid of ...'). The verb 'کن' issues the command to render the world empty of those beings/entities.
segments.s109-g39: 'کمر بست' is an idiom: past tense 'بست' (bound) with 'کمر' (waist) = 'girded his loins'. The conjunction 'و' links the next action 'رفت' = 'he went/left'. The prepositional phrase 'از بر شاه گیو' = 'from before the king Giv' (here 'از بر' = 'from the presence of').
segments.s109-g40: Literal sequence 'ز لشکر گزین کرد گردان نیو' = 'from the army (ز لشکر) he selected (گزین کرد) the troop (گردان) Niu'. Here 'گزین کرد' functions as 'chose/selected' (گزین + auxiliary کرد), and the noun phrase 'گردان نیو' names the unit chosen—'نیو' appears as a proper name/identifier of that troop.
segments.s109-g97: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. تاریک شد is a past-tense verbal phrase 'became dark' (شد = past of شدن). چشم کاووس شاه is the possessed noun phrase 'the eye/sight of Kavus the king' placed after the verb in the poetic word order (verb before subject/possessor).
segments.s109-g98: بد آمد is an impersonal idiom meaning 'ill/unfavorable came' or 'misfortune befell'. ز here means 'because of' (causal), کردار او 'his conduct' is the cause, and بر سپاه means 'upon the army' ('بر' = upon/against). The clause states the result that the army suffered on account of his actions.
segments.s109-g99: همه modifies the following nouns: 'all the treasure' (همه گنج). تاراج is used as a noun/participial idea 'plunder' (i.e. was plundered). و links the second coordinated phrase لشکر اسیر, where اسیر functions as predicate/adjective 'taken captive' (the verb 'was' is omitted, a common poetic ellipsis).
segments.s109-g100: A verbless (or elliptic) clause: جوان دولت و بخت are the subjects ('youth, power/sovereignty, and fortune'), برگشت is the past verb 'returned' (or 'turned') and پیر is the predicate adjective 'old' — literally 'youth ... returned old' (i.e. they were lost/turned into age). The sequence places the items before the verb for emphasis.
segments.s109-g101: همه داستان is the direct object 'all the tales'. یاد باید گرفت is an impersonal/periphrastic construction where یاد گرفتن ('to learn/remember') appears with باید 'must' and گرفت (past of گرفتن) — together meaning 'must be learned' or 'one must learn'. The order (object before the verb) is typical of classical/poetic Persian.
segments.s109-g102: که introduces the content/result clause 'that/such that'. خیره نماید is a causative/valence-raising phrase: خیره ('staring, astonished') with نماید (present/subjunctive of نمودن used here as 'makes/shows') — 'makes (one) stare/astonished'. شگفت از شگفت is a doubled noun phrase 'wonder upon wonder' (از = 'from/of' here giving the sense 'after/one upon another').
segments.s109-g103: سپهبد is the speaker's title. چنین گفت is a fixed phrase 'said thus/so' (چنین modifying گفت). چون دید رنج is a temporal clause 'when he saw the hardship' — چون used in the temporal sense 'when' and دید is simple past 'saw', with رنج as the object.
segments.s109-g104: که introduces the reported content: دستور بیدار is a noun phrase 'an alert/vigilant command/order' (دستور = command/order; بیدار = awake/alert). بهتر is the comparative 'better', and ز گنج uses poetic z (from/than) in a comparative construction meaning 'than treasure' (گنج = treasure). Together: 'an alert command is better than treasure.'
segments.s109-g57: به کاووس بردند is a simple past clause: بردند is the 3rd-person-plural past of بردن 'to take/carry' and takes the goal introduced by به (to). از او آگهی is a noun phrase functioning as the thing carried: از here means 'about/concerning' (source or topic) and آگهی 'information/news'. Word order is object/topic after the verb, as common in Ferdowsi's epic diction.
segments.s109-g58: ازان خرمی جای و آن فرهی is a compact genitive/description: ازان 'of that' (= از + آن) marks source/possession; خرمی جای 'the delight/pleasantness of the place' (خرمی noun + جای 'place'); و آن فرهی 'and that nobility/grace' links a second nominal attribute. The whole phrase appends as the content of آگهی in the previous segment.
segments.s109-g59: همی گفت is a habitual/continuous past construction: همی adds the sense 'kept/continued' to گفت (past tense of گفتن 'to say'). خرم زیاد is an exclamatory/qualifying phrase ('very joyful/delightful') modifying what was said. آنک گفت here introduces the reported cause/statement: آنک = 'that/for/inasmuch as' and the following گفت is the verb 'he said' (the lexical list given treats گفت as a noun, so the verbal sense is not listed there).
segments.s109-g60: که introduces the reported content clause: مازندران را is topicalized with the object marker را (typical in poetic word-order), بهشتست is a fused predicate (بهشت + -ست = 'is Paradise' / 'is like Paradise'), and جفت follows as a nominative noun meaning 'pair/counterpart' — i.e. 'Mazandaran is a twin/counterpart of Paradise.' The clause reads literally 'that Mazandaran is Paradise's pair.'
segments.s109-g61: همه شهر is the subject phrase 'the whole city.' گویی here is parenthetical and means 'it seems / one would say' (a literary use of گوئی); مگر in this clause functions as an intensifying/exclamatory particle ('as if' / 'indeed'), and بتکده‌ست is the predicate 'is an idol‑temple' (بتکده + -ست). The clause therefore literally reads 'the whole city, it seems, is as if an idol‑temple.'
segments.s109-g62: This short prepositional/verb cluster describes how the city is adorned: ز دیبای چین = 'from (or of) Chinese brocade' (ز is the poetic form of از), بر گل = 'upon the flowers', آذین زدست is the verbal idea 'they ornamented/they set decorations' (آذین زدن = to decorate; زدست here is analysed as an instrumental/agentive expression 'by hand' in the supplied senses). In sum: 'they adorned the flowers with Chinese brocade by hand.'
segments.s109-g63: بتان بهشتند is a nominal sentence: بتان (idols/beauties) as the subject and بهشتند (they are Paradise) as the predicate. گویی again is parenthetical 'it seems,' and درست functions as an intensifier meaning 'truly' or 'indeed.' The sequence states an impression: 'The idols are like Paradise, it seems — indeed.'
segments.s109-g64: به گلنارشان = 'upon their golnār (pomegranate‑blossoms)' with به marking direction/target; روی رضوان = 'the face of Ridwan/Paradise' (روی = face, رضوان = Paradise personified), and بشست is the past verb 'he washed.' Personification: Paradise (رضوان) is the agent who 'washed its face' upon their blossoms — a poetic way of saying their faces are so fair that Paradise itself has washed there.
segments.s109-g105: 'به سختی' is an adverbial phrase meaning “with difficulty / hardly.” The time clause 'چو یک هفته اندر کشید' uses چو + time phrase + فعل (کشید) to mean 'when/about/as a week passed' or 'it lasted about a week'; کشید here is an archaic verb meaning 'to last / to endure' (past). The whole segment functions as a temporal/adverbial clause modifying the following statement.
segments.s109-g106: 'به دیده' is a locative/instrumental phrase 'to/with the eye' (i.e. 'in sight'). 'ز ایرانیان' is a prepositional/genitive phrase 'of/from the Iranians.' 'کس' is the indefinite pronoun placed before the verb and, with the negative verb 'ندید' (past, negative), yields 'saw no one.' Word order is typical Persian: adverbial/locative phrase + source phrase + object + negative past verb.
segments.s109-g107: 'بهشتم' (= در بهشتم) is a locative adverbial 'in my paradise.' 'بغرید' is a past tense verb 'roared' (بغرید = غریدن/بغریدن in an archaic/poetic form). The subject 'دیو سپید' (the white demon) follows the verb, a common poetic inversion (V S rather than S V).
segments.s109-g108: The phrase begins with a vocative/exclamatory address 'که‌ای' (poetic 'که ای' = 'O'), then the noun 'شاه' 'king' modified by the adjective 'بی‌بر' 'without offspring.' 'به کردار بید' is a predicate phrase literally 'by/with regard to (your) conduct, (you are) a willow' — the copula is omitted (typical in poetry) and the metaphor 'بید' (willow) describes the king's behavior. 'به کردار' is an adverbial/instrumental phrase 'in/by conduct.'
segments.s109-g109: 'همی' is an adverb meaning 'always/continually' placed before the clause. 'برتری را' is a definite object ('superiority' with the object marker را). 'بیاراستی' is a past 2nd‑person singular verb (archaic form) meaning 'you made ready / you adorned / you secured' — the verb completes the object 'برتری را.'
segments.s109-g110: 'چراگاه مازندران' is a noun phrase 'the pasture (چراگاه) of Mazandaran' (genitive relation: domain after head). 'خواستی' is the past 2nd‑person singular of 'خواستن' meaning 'you wanted / you sought' and is the main verb of this clause; word order is typical S(phrase) + V.
segments.s109-g111: همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle here that belongs with the verb later (it commonly marks ongoing or habitual action in classical Persian and anticipates بدیدی). نیروی خویش is a possessive noun phrase: نیروی (the noun 'force/strength' with the ezāfe relation implicit) + خویش written as two pieces here (خوی + ش), where ش is the possessive enclitic 'his/her/its/one's own'. چون introduces a simile ('like'), followed by پیل مست ('an elephant, drunken/intoxicated'), with the adjective مست placed after پیل as is normal in older Persian poetry.
segments.s109-g112: بدیدی is a past-tense verb form (here functioning as 'showed/displayed' in context) and links back to the earlier همی (the combined phrase means 'you were showing/you displayed'). و is the coordinator 'and'. کس را uses کس 'anyone/someone' as the indirect object marked by the object marker را. ندادی is the past negative of دادن ('you did not give'), with the negative prefix ن-. تو appears after the verb for emphasis ('you' as the (stressed) subject), and دست ('hand') is the noun that here carries the idiomatic sense 'hand/help/assistance' — i.e. 'you did not give your hand to anyone'.
segments.s109-g1: چو is a temporal subordinating particle meaning 'when' introducing the clause. زال سپهبد is a name + title (Zāl, the spahbed). ز پهلو برفت is a verb phrase 'he departed from the flank' with ز (poetic 'from') before پهلو ('flank, side') and برفت as a past-form of رفتن; the whole clause means 'when Zāl the spahbed departed from the flank.' Word order places the temporal particle at the start and the verb at the end, as is common in classical Persian.
segments.s109-g2: دمادم is an adverb 'constantly, repeatedly.' سپه (the host/army) is the patient/theme; روی بنهاد is a compound verbal phrase meaning 'set (its) face' — i.e. 'set (its) direction' or 'put in motion' (روی 'face' + بنهاد past of نهادن 'to place/put'). و connects the verbs; تفت is a short verb here meaning a further action of advancing/striking (poetic usage). Together the line says he repeatedly put the army’s face forward and pressed on. Note some forms are poetic; verb forms follow classical past placement.
segments.s109-g3: به طوس و به گودرز marks the indirect objects 'to Tous and to Goudarz' (the preposition به before each name). فرمود is the past-tense verb 'he said/commanded'; شاه following the verb is the subject 'the king' placed after the verb for stylistic emphasis (inversion common in epic diction). The sequence literally reads 'to Tous and to Goudarz the king said,' i.e. 'the king said to Tous and Goudarz.'
segments.s109-g4: کشیدن سپه is a verbal-nominal construction where کشیدن (a masdar/infinitive form) means 'to move on / to draw/march' and سپه is its object 'the host/army' — 'to march the army.' سر نهادن is another verbal phrase where سر ('head') + نهادن ('to place') means 'to set (its) head' i.e. 'to put it/them on the road' (figuratively: to begin the march). به راه is the prepositional phrase 'onto the road' or 'on the way.' These infinitival/gerundial forms report the content of the king's order.
segments.s109-g5: چو شب روز شد is a temporal clause 'when night became day' (چو = 'when', شب = 'night', روز = 'day', شد = past 'became'). شاه و جنگ‌آوران follows as the agent(s) of the main clause: 'the king and the warriors' — the conjunction and noun phrase are placed after the time clause, a common classical order.
segments.s109-g6: نهادند is the past plural of نهادن 'they placed/put' (here used idiomatically with سر: 'they set (their) heads'). سر is the direct object 'head' used idiomatically (set their faces/direction). سوی مازندران is a prepositional phrase 'toward Mazandaran' indicating direction. Altogether: 'they set their faces toward Mazandaran' = 'they headed for Mazandaran.'
segments.s109-g7: به میلاد بسپرد ایران‌زمین is a ditransitive construction where بسپرد (past of سپردن 'to entrust/hand over') governs an indirect object marked by به (here 'to Milad') and a direct object ایران‌زمین ('the land of Iran' — the thing entrusted). Word order places the indirect object first for emphasis: 'he entrusted (it) to Milad, the land of Iran.'
segments.s109-g8: This segment lists the items handed over: کلید در گنج ('the key of/to the treasure') followed by و تاج و نگین ('and the crown and the gem'). کلید is the noun 'key' and در گنج is a modifier specifying which key ('to/in the treasure'); the conjunction و links the subsequent nouns. These are the direct objects/items associated with the entrusting action in the previous segment.
segments.s109-g25: همه شب is a temporal adverbial meaning “the whole night.” می‌و (an archaic/poetic spelling) names the wine/fruit used to supply the feast and functions as the matter with which they adorned the gathering. آراستند is past tense, 3rd person plural (they adorned/arranged), placed at the end as the main verb; the clause literally reads “they adorned the gathering with wine all night.”
segments.s109-g26: به شبگیر is a temporal prepositional phrase meaning ‘at the turning/close of the night’ (poetic for dawn/when the night ends). کز is the relative/subordinating particle ‘that/who,’ introducing the subordinate clause کز خواب برخاستند; خواب is ‘sleep,’ and برخاستند is the past 3rd‑person plural ‘they rose.’
segments.s109-g27: پراگنده (‘scattered’) is used adverbially before the locative phrase نزدیک شاه (‘near/close to the king’). آمدند is the main verb in past 3rd‑person plural (‘they came’), so the whole clause means ‘they approached the king in scattered fashion.’
segments.s109-g28: کمر بسته is a participial/adjectival circumstantial phrase literally ‘having bound their girdles’ (i.e., girded at the waist, ready for action). The coordinate phrase و با کلاه آمدند adds ‘and they came with helmets’; با introduces the instrument/attire. آمدند remains the main verb governing these accompanying phrases.
segments.s109-g29: بفرمود is an honorific past verb meaning ‘he commanded/said.’ پس is temporal/adverbial ‘then.’ گیو را شهریار uses the object marker را with a nominal predicate (شهریار) — a common classical construction meaning ‘he made Giv shahriar’ (the verb ‘to make’ is implied by بفرمود here).
segments.s109-g30: دوباره is an adverb ‘again.’ ز لشکر = ‘from the army’ (z is the poetic form of from/of). گزیدن is the infinitive ‘to choose/select’ and functions as the verbal complement of the command (i.e., he ordered Giv to select a thousand from the troops); the numeral هزار is the object of گزیدن (not listed among the annotated words).
segments.s109-g31: کسی is an indefinite pronoun ‘someone/anyone.’ کاو is an archaic/poetic form functioning here as a relative pronoun ‘who’ (note: the provided lemma senses do not match this usage). گراید is the present/subjunctive/optative form ‘should turn/tend/attempt,’ and به گرز گران is a prepositional phrase ‘to/toward the heavy mace’ (گرز = mace, گران = heavy). The clause is conditional/hypothetical.
segments.s109-g32: گشاینده is a present‑participle noun ‘opener’ used attributively (postpositive) to specify the function of the mace. شهر مازندران is the noun phrase ‘the city of Mazandaran’ (classical poetry often omits the ezafe linking these two nouns). Together the phrase describes the mace as ‘the opener/revealer of the city of Mazandaran.’
segments.s109-g41: بشد is a literary past narrative/optative verb meaning 'it came to pass' or 'it happened' and here introduces a result clause with تا. تا functions as a connective 'that/so that' introducing the content that follows. در شهر مازندران is a locative prepositional phrase (در + noun) 'in the city of Mazandaran' placed after تا to give the location of the ensuing action.
segments.s109-g42: ببارید is a classical past intransitive verb 'it rained/fell' used figuratively; in Persian poetry the verb is placed before its subject for emphasis. شمشیر و گرز گران is the compound subject following the verb: two coordinated nouns 'sword and mace' with the adjective گران ('heavy' / 'weighty') postpositive, describing the weapons.
segments.s109-g43: زن و کودک و مرد is a simple coordination 'woman and child and man' listing people; Persian lists often place the coordinating conjunction و between items. با introduces a circumstantial phrase 'with' and دستوار (here 'steady' / 'firm') describes their state or manner; the whole phrase modifies the context (how they were).
segments.s109-g44: نیافت is the past negative of یافتن ('to find') and here functions impersonally: 'did not find/obtain' (i.e. 'none escaped'). از introduces the source/agent phrase 'from the head/point of the sword' (از + سر تیغ). او is the third-person pronoun 'his/he' (the sword's owner), and زینهار is a final interjection 'beware!' or a warning appended to the clause.
segments.s109-g45: همی before a verb is an older continuous/aspective particle meaning 'was (doing)'. همی کرد غارت is therefore 'he was carrying out plunder' (کرد as the lexical verb 'to do' with غارت as its object). The second clause همی سوخت شهر uses the same aspectual همی with سوخت (past 'burned') and the noun شهر as subject; the two short clauses are juxtaposed for rapid narrative motion.
segments.s109-g46: بپالود is a literary past verb (here used transitively) meaning roughly 'treated/laid/put' in classical usage; بر جای is a fixed prepositional phrase 'in place of / instead of' (بر + جای). تریاک is the noun 'opium' (what is being replaced) and زهر 'poison' is the item placed 'in place of' it; the word order places the verb first and the prepositional complement after it.
segments.s109-g47: یکی here is the indefinite 'once / a certain (time/person)' marking a narrative opening. چون introduces a simile 'like/as'. بهشت برین is a compound: بهشت 'paradise' plus the superlative-forming element برین ('supreme / highest'), together 'the highest paradise'. شهر is the object 'the city' and دید is the past verb 'saw'.
segments.s109-g48: پر از is a fixed expression 'full of' (پر 'full' + از 'of'). خرمی is the noun 'mirth/delight/bloom' describing what the place was full of. بر درش is a locative phrase 'on/at its gate' (بر + در + possessive suffix ش 'its'). بهر here functions adverbially 'for / toward' but in this collocation بهر دید is an idiomatic sequence meaning 'he saw (it) at / toward'—practically, 'he saw at its gate ...'. دید is the past verb 'saw'.
segments.s109-g121: "سران را همه بندها ساختند" is a transitive past tense clause with the verb ساختند (they made) at the end. "سران را" uses را as the definite/direct-object marker, so "سران" (the chiefs/leaders) is the patient of ساختند. "همه" modifies بندها (all the fetters). Word order (object + را + modifier + plural noun + verb) is normal in older/poetic Persian: the thing made (all the fetters) is said with its indirect object (the chiefs) marked by را before the verb.
segments.s109-g122: "چو از بند و بستن بپرداختند" is a temporal subordinate clause introduced by چو (here 'when'). از + noun phrase (بند و بستن, 'the binding and the fastening/tying') names the activity; بپرداختند is a past-tense verb whose older/poetic sense can mean 'ceased from / were done with' or 'turned away from (that task)'. Taken together the clause means 'when they had finished/left off from binding and fastening.'
segments.s109-g123: "خورش دادشان اندکی جان سپوز" has خورش (a ration/food) as the thing given and دادشان (gave them) as the verb + object clitic (شان). اندکی ('a little') qualifies the amount. "جان سپوز" here functions as a phrase meaning 'to preserve life' (an epithet/phrase attached to the giving) — in the manuscript tradition سپوز can appear as an epithet or name used here to indicate life-sustaining/keeping; grammatically it is in apposition to the small provision that was given.
segments.s109-g124: "بدان تا گذارند روزی به‌روز" begins with the prefixed form بدان (ب + دان) functioning as 'by that / thereby' or 'with that', introducing purpose: تا ('so that') + گذارند (a subjunctive/optative sense 'they may pass/they may live through') + روزی به‌روز ('day by day'). Together this yields 'by that, so that they may live/bring themselves through from day to day.' Note the older/poetic use of گذارند for surviving/passing time.
segments.s109-g125: "ازان پس همه گنج شاه جهان" is a temporal/discourse phrase: ازان پس ('after that' / 'thereafter') followed by the noun phrase همه گنج شاه جهان (all the treasure of the king of the world). Note the genitive chain (گنجِ شاهِ جهان) is compacted without ezāfe in epic verse, a common poetic compression.
segments.s109-g126: "چه از تاج یاقوت و گرز گران" is a partitive/enumerative clause introduced by چه ('whether / such as / be it') followed by examples introduced with از ('of / from'): تاج یاقوت ('a crown of ruby') و گرز گران ('and a heavy/precious mace'). The construction lists items included in the treasure; چه here plays the role 'whether/ be it ... or ...'.
segments.s109-g127: "سپرد آنچ دید از کران تا کران" places the verb سپرد (he entrusted/handed over) at the front, with its object expressed as آنچ دید ('that which he saw'). "از کران تا کران" is an idiom meaning 'from edge to edge' (i.e., throughout, everywhere). The clause therefore means 'he handed over whatever he had seen all across (the realm).' Note the relative/nominal clause structure: آنچ (that which) + دید (he saw).
segments.s109-g128: "به ارژنگ سالار مازندران" is the dative phrase marking the recipient: به ('to') + ارژنگ (personal name) + سالار (title 'commander/chief') + مازندران (place/name of the realm). Put together: 'to Arzhang, the commander of Mazandaran.' The name and title form a nominal group in apposition (name + title + region).
segments.s109-g17: "به جایی که پنهان شود آفتاب" is a prepositional + noun + relative clause. به marks direction/goal ('to/at'); جایی is 'a place'; که introduces a relative clause ('where/that'); پنهان شود is a subjunctive/passive verbal phrase 'be hidden' (شود here functions as 'be' in the sense of becoming/being hidden); آفتاب, although placed after the verb for poetic emphasis, is the subject of پنهان‌شود ('the sun' that hides).
segments.s109-g18: بدان جایگه = به + (دان) + جایگه, a demonstrative locative phrase 'to/at that place' (دان here functions as the deictic element 'that'); ساخت is simple past 'he made/built'; آرام و خواب are coordinated description/predicates ('calm' / 'rest' and 'sleep'), so the clause says he made that place to be calm and for sleeping. Word order: locative phrase precedes the verb, then descriptive nouns/adjectives follow.
segments.s109-g19: کجا at the front is a locative/interrogative adverb here used expository 'where'; جای دیوان forms 'the place/dwelling of the divs' (دیوان = divs/demons in epic usage); دژخیم is an adjectival epithet 'cruel/tormenting/oppressive' modifying دیوان; بود is past 'was' placed at the end as the copula showing existence/location.
segments.s109-g20: بدان جایگه = 'in/at that place' (demonstrative locative); the construction پیل را بیم بود is an impersonal/possessive pattern meaning 'there was fear for the elephant' or idiomatically 'the elephant was afraid' — پیل را marks the affected/experiencer (with را as topic/affected-mark), بیم is the noun 'fear', and بود is the past copula 'was.'
segments.s109-g21: بگسترد is simple past 'he spread/laid out'; زربفت is the direct object 'gold-brocade' (a cloth); بر indicates the surface 'over/upon'; میش is 'ewe/sheep' (poetic); سار here follows میش as a poetic/nominal element attached to the flock — the exact nuance of سار in this compound is specialized/poetic (the listed senses do not directly give 'flock'), so the sequence means 'over the ewes' or 'over the flock of ewes.'
segments.s109-g22: هوا is the subject 'the air'; پر ز + noun = 'full of ...' (پر = 'full (of)'); ز is the poetic preposition 'of/from'; بوی is 'scent'; از می is 'from/of wine' (می = wine); خوشگوار is a predicative adjective 'pleasant' or 'agreeably fragrant.' The whole phrase means 'the air, full of the scent of wine, was pleasant/fragrant.' Note the poetic order (prepositional phrase inserted before the predicate adjective).
segments.s109-g23: همه پهلوانان is a noun phrase 'all the champions'; فرخنده is an adjective 'auspicious/fortunate' modifying them; پی here means 'following/attending' (i.e., 'in attendance' or 'present'), so together the phrase describes the assembled heroes as 'all the fortunate champions in attendance.'
segments.s109-g24: نشستند is past plural 'they sat down' (third-person plural). بر تخت = 'upon the throne'; کاووس کی is a name + royal title construction: کاووس is the proper name Kavus and کی is the regal epithet/title (appearing after the name in epic usage), so تخت کاووس کی = 'the throne of King Kavus.' Word order places the preposition + object before the name-title phrase; the verb sits at the clause head (past) as the action.
segments.s109-g129: بر + شاه + رو is a compact locative phrase meaning 'turn the face toward the king' (بر ... رو = 'toward the king'). گفت is a past tense verb 'said'. The conjunction و links to the following imperative phrase; او را بگوی is an object (او) with the definite/object marker را, plus بگوی (imperative/jussive form of گفتن/گو) meaning 'tell him'. So the clause reads, 'He turned to the king and said (to someone): tell him...'.
segments.s109-g130: که introduces the reported content ('that' / 'so that'). ز here means 'from' / 'on account of' and governs آهرمن (Ahriman). اکنون = 'now'. بهانه = 'excuse, pretext'. مجوی is a negative jussive/imperative (classical form) meaning 'do not seek' (second person singular). The whole subordinate clause gives the command: 'that he not now seek a pretext from Ahriman.'
segments.s109-g131: همه پهلوانان ایران و شاه is a coordinated subject: 'all the champions of Iran and the king.' The adjective/quantifier همه scopes over پهلوانان and the coordination adds شاه, so both groups are the participants of the following predicate.
segments.s109-g132: This line uses correlative negation with نه ... نه ...: 'neither ... nor ...'. بینند is the classical-present/optative form 'they see' (here simple present). روشن is a postpositive adjective 'bright' modifying خورشید. The second negated item is ماه 'the moon'. Literally: 'they see neither the sun bright nor the moon.'
segments.s109-g133: به + infinitive (کشتن) + نکردم is a classical periphrastic negation meaning 'I did not kill' (literally 'to kill I did not'). برو is the imperative 'go'. بر before نهیب here is adverbial 'with' or 'upon' and نهیب is a strong rebuke or thunderous cry; together بْرو بر نهیب = 'go and rebuke him / give him a sharp reprimand.'
segments.s109-g134: بدان is the prefix ب- + دان forming an imperative 'know / tell (him)' (here 'tell him'). تا is purposive 'so that' and introduces a subordinate clause with بداند (subjunctive/optative third person) 'he may know'. فراز و نشیب is an idiomatic pair meaning 'the ups and downs' or 'the rises and falls.'
segments.s109-g135: به here functions adverbially 'by/through' (indicating cause or means). زاری و سختی 'lamentation and hardship' is the instrumental/causal phrase. برآیدش is the present/classical-form verb برآییدن ('arise') with the enclitic -ش 'to him/for him', so 'his awareness arises/returns'. هوش 'awareness, mind' is the thing that (to) him arises.
segments.s109-g136: کسی نیز ننهد is a negative generalization: with ننهد (negative present of نهاد used as 'place/put') the phrase means 'no one even places' (i.e. 'no one pays heed'). برین is composition of بر + این (here written بر + ین) meaning 'on this'; کار 'matter/affair'; گوش 'ear' used idiomatically for 'attention'. So 'no one lends an ear to this matter.'
segments.s109-g49: به هر برزنی is a locative prepositional phrase: به + هر + برزنی = 'at/for every village-square' (classical Persian often uses به where modern Persian would use در). بر فزون از هزار is a compact comparative phrase: بر + فزون + از + هزار = 'over/more than a thousand' (بر here gives the sense 'over/above' followed by فزون 'more' + از 'than'). The copula/verb is omitted (understood 'there were').
segments.s109-g50: پرستار here means 'worshipper/adorer' (people gathered in devotional posture). با طوق و با گوشوار is a coordinate instrumental phrase 'with a necklace and with an earring(s)', with repeated با for rhythmic/poetic emphasis; the whole phrase modifies پرستار.
segments.s109-g51: پرستنده is an agent noun 'worshipper/ devotee'. زین بیشتر is a comparative phrase meaning 'more than these' or 'besides these, more'; here زین is an archaic/poetic element (classical زین = 'than this' or a pointing word), so the exact sense is irregular in the supplied lexicon. با کلاه 'with hat(s)' describes their headgear.
segments.s109-g52: به چهره = 'in/with regard to appearance' (به used adverbially). به کردار = 'in the manner/like' (کردار here means 'manner/appearance' rather than a moral 'deed'). تابنده ماه is a noun phrase 'the shining moon' with تابنده as a participial adjective modifying ماه; the sequence describes their faces as moonlike.
segments.s109-g53: به هر جای is a locative phrase 'in/at every place'. گنجی پراگنده زر is a nominal group: گنجی (treasure) + پراگنده (scattered) + زر (gold) — the verb 'was/were' is omitted and understood, a common classical Persian ellipse.
segments.s109-g54: به یک جای = 'in one place/spot'. دینار سرخ و گهر is a simple coordination 'a red dinar and a gem' (دینار = coin). Again the existence verb is omitted: the phrase lists valuables found in one spot.
segments.s109-g55: بی‌اندازه is an adverb 'excessively/countless'. گرد اندرش = 'around him' where گرد is an adverb/prepositional adverb 'about/around' and اندر + ش is a classical locative + 3rd-person possessive clitic 'in/at his ...'. چارپای is the noun 'four-footed animal(s)/beasts' and is the subject of the understood verb 'were gathered/there were'.
segments.s109-g56: بهشتیست is an adjectival predicate 'it is paradisal/heavenly' (پیوندِ صفتی with the archaic copula -ست). گفتی here is a parenthetical/quotative formula 'one might say' or 'you could say' (an idiomatic classical use not matched by the provided senses). همیدون appears to be a place-name (so 'call it Hamīdūn'); به جای is locative 'to the place' meaning 'call the place thus.'
segments.s109-g73: بگویش is the imperative form بگو (second-person singular “tell”) plus the enclitic -یش (archaic/literary for ‘to him’), so ‘tell him’. که is the subordinating conjunction “that” introducing the reported clause. آمد is a simple past, 3rd-person singular (“he/it came”); here it reports the arrival. به is the preposition indicating motion/toward (here “to/into”), and مازندران is the place-name object of that preposition (“Mazandaran”).
segments.s109-g74: بغارت is here an adverbial phrase meaning ‘for plunder’ (classical usage بغارت = به غارت). از is the preposition “from.” ایران is the source/place “Iran.” سپاهی functions as a collective noun/adjective meaning an armed force or host (lit. ‘troops’). گران is an adjective ‘heavy/weighty’ used metaphorically = ‘powerful/formidable’. The whole segment modifies آمد (‘came… for plunder, from Iran, a formidable army’).
segments.s109-g75: جهانجوی is an epithet (compound adjective) ‘world-seeking’ placed before the name. کاووس is the proper name ‘Kavus.’ شان here is the third-person plural possessive/enclitic ‘their’ (an honorific/possessive referring to his followers), linking Kavus to the following phrase as their leader. پیش‌رو is a compound meaning ‘in front / at the head’ (پیش = before/in front; رو as part of the compound gives the sense ‘forward/ahead’). Grammatically this unit describes Kavus’ position as leader or vanguard.
segments.s109-g76: یکی is the indefinite numeral/particle ‘one’ or ‘a certain,’ introducing a single host. لشگری is a noun ‘an army/host’ (lshgr + the locutional ending -ی). جنگ here is the noun ‘war’ and combines with سازان to form a compound meaning ‘those who make war’ (fighters/war‑makers). سازان is the agentive plural ‘makers/creators’ (here ‘those who make/prepare war’). نو is the adjective ‘new,’ describing the army as newly raised or new in formation.
segments.s109-g77: کنون‌گر is an archaic conditional particle/phrase meaning ‘if now’ or ‘now if’ (کنون = now; -گر used to form conditions). نباشی is the negative present/subjunctive (2nd-person singular) of بودن ‘to be’ used in the protasis: ‘if you are not.’ تو is the emphatic pronoun ‘you.’ فریادرس is a noun ‘rescuer/succorer,’ so the clause literally reads ‘if now you are not a rescuer.’
segments.s109-g78: نبینی is a negative present/subjunctive or predictive 2nd‑person form of دیدن (‘you will not see’ / ‘you shall not see’) used here as the apodosis (result) of the conditional. بمازندران is the fused prepositional phrase ب + مازندران = ‘in/at Mazandaran.’ زنده is the adjective ‘alive’ and کس is the indefinite noun ‘anyone/person,’ so together ‘you will see no living person in Mazandaran.’ Note the negative scope: نبینی negates the existence/visibility of زنده کس.
segments.s109-g79: چو is the temporal conjunction ‘when.’ بشنید is the simple past 3rd-person singular ‘he heard.’ پیغام is the direct object ‘message.’ سنجه here appears in the text as a noun that functions like a subject/name in this clause; given the lemma list it may be either a proper name or an otherwise non‑literal word, but syntactically it stands before نهفت. نهفت is the past of نهفتن ‘to hide/conceal’ (3rd-person singular ‘he hid’ or ‘it was hidden’). The clause reads ‘when he heard the message, (Sanjeh) hid (it / himself).’
segments.s109-g80: بر is the preposition ‘to/toward’ marking the indirect object. دیو is ‘the demon.’ پیغام شه is a genitive construction ‘the king’s message’ (پیغام = message; شه = king). بازگفت is a past-tense verb ‘he re‑told / reported back’ (3rd-person singular). Word order is typical classical Persian: indirect object phrase (بر دیو) + direct object (پیغام شه) + verb (بازگفت) = ‘he reported the king’s message to the demon.’
segments.s109-g9: بدو is the dative phrase 'to him' (به + او) placed before the reported speech. گفت‌گر is the compound of گفت + گر (the verb + conditional particle 'if') introducing the subordinate clause; the particle گر attaches to گفت to mean 'he said, if...'. دشمن آید پدید is the subordinate clause with دشمن (subject) + آید (present/past-like verb 'comes/appears') + پدید (poetic adverb 'forth, appear'), so literally 'if the enemy comes forth.'
segments.s109-g10: ترا را is the object 'you' (ت + را = تو را) with the object marker را; تیغ کینه is a genitive phrase 'sword of hatred/vengeance' (noun + modifier). بباید is the impersonal modal 'it is necessary / you must', followed by کشید (infinitival/verb) forming the verbal complex 'must draw' — i.e. 'you must draw the sword of vengeance.' Word order places the object phrase before the modal + verb, a common poetic ordering.
segments.s109-g11: ز هر بد is a prepositional phrase 'from every ill' (ز = from, هر = every, بد = bad/evil). به زال و به رستم پناه uses به + name twice to mark 'to/with Zal and with Rostam' and پناه (refuge) is used elliptically (the verb 'seek/take' is omitted in poetic register) to mean 'seek refuge with Zal and Rostam.'
segments.s109-g12: که introduces a relative clause referring back to those named (Zal and Rostam). پشت سپاهند literally 'they are the back/support of the army' (پشت = support/rear, سپاهند = poetic contraction of سپاه‌اند 'they are an army' or 'they are for the army'). و زیبای گاه is a nominative apposition 'and the beauty/ornament of the time' (زیبای = 'of beauty' or adjectival genitive modifier to گاه = 'time/age').
segments.s109-g13: دگر روز 'another day' (time phrase) comes before the verb for temporal emphasis. برخاست is the past tense 'arose/rose up' and آوای کوس is the subject in genitive 'the sound (آوا) of the drum (کوس)'; verb preceding subject is a common poetic order.
segments.s109-g14: سپه را uses the object marker را (definite object 'the army') placed before the verb for emphasis. همی راند is an iterative/progressive past 'kept driving/was driving', and گودرز و طوس are the compound subject following the verb: 'Goudarz and Tous (the heroes) were driving the army.'
segments.s109-g15: همی رفت is the iterative past 'kept going / went on'. کاووس is the subject (Kavus) and لشکر فروز is a postpositive epithet 'the glorious army' (لشکر = army, فروز = glorious/bright) modifying it; the sequence means 'Kavus proceeded with his glorious army.'
segments.s109-g16: بزد گاه is an idiomatic poetic expression meaning 'he struck a place' i.e. 'he made camp' (بزد used with گاه to indicate setting up a position). بر پیش کوه اسپروز is a locative phrase 'before/in front of Mount Esporoz' (بر + پیش = in front of; کوه اسپروز = Mount Esporoz). The whole clause describes where he made camp.
segments.s109-g113: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. با تاج و با تخت uses repeated با ('with') for emphasis: 'with crown and with throne'. نشکیفتی is an archaic/poetic past second‑person form meaning 'you took a seat / you rose (to power)' (here referring to assuming the throne); it follows the prepositional phrases and completes the temporal clause.
segments.s109-g114: خرد را is the definite object 'wisdom' marked by را. بدین‌گونه is an adverbial 'in this way / thus'. بفریفتی is a past tense (poetic) 2nd‑person form 'you deceived' (ب‑ prefix here is part of the classical verbal form); together the clause says 'you deceived wisdom thus.'
segments.s109-g115: کنون is the temporal adverb 'now'. آنچ is the relative 'that which'. اندر is the preposition 'in/within'. The phrase اندر خور کار تست is idiomatic/poetic: کار 'work/affair' is clear, and the whole chunk means roughly 'in your sphere/affair' (تست is an archaic possessive/predicate element meaning 'is yours'). The clause sets the context for what follows ('now that which in your work belongs to you...').
segments.s109-g116: دلت is دل + 2nd‑person suffix ت = 'your heart'. یافت is past tense 'found/obtained' (here 'obtained'). آن آرزوها 'those wishes' is the object; که introduces a relative clause. جست is the past of جستن 'to seek' and here forms the relative 'which you sought' (subject of جست is understood from context).
segments.s109-g117: ازان = 'from those' (demonstrative + preposition). نره is an adjective meaning 'manly/virile' modifying the following noun. دیوان in epic context denotes 'divs' (demons/giants) rather than the modern senses listed; خنجرگذار is a compound noun 'dagger‑bearer / dagger‑striker' describing the kind of fighters chosen.
segments.s109-g118: گزین کرد is a two‑word verbal sequence 'he chose / he appointed' (گزین = choose, کرد = did/made — standard past narration). جنگی is a noun/adjective 'fighters / warlike men' serving as the object; the following numeric phrase (ده و دوهزار) gives the quantity — 'ten and two‑thousand' (classical numeric ordering, i.e. 2,010). و simply coordinates the numerals; دوهزار = 'two thousand'.
segments.s109-g119: بر ایرانیان is a prepositional phrase 'over/on the Iranians' (بر = over). بر نگهدار کرد is 'he set/placed a guardian' — نگهدار 'guardian, keeper' and کرد 'he made/did' (i.e. appointed). The doubled بر marks the role/placement: 'appointed over the Iranians a guardian.'
segments.s109-g120: سر 'head' begins the noun phrase 'سر سرکشان' = 'the heads of the سرکشان' (سرکشان here denotes proud/rebellious chiefs in context — the lemma senses given don't capture this epic usage). پر ز تیمار is a fixed expression 'full of care/worry' (پر = full (of), ز = from/because of, تیمار = care). کرد closes the clause: 'he made (their) heads full of care' (past tense).
segments.s109-g81: This is a compact past‑tense clause: چنين ('thus') modifies the whole reply; پاسخش is پاسخ + enclitic -ش ('his/its answer'); داد is the simple past of دادن ('gave/said'). دیو سپید is noun + postpositive adjective (typical Persian order) meaning 'the White Demon' — the epithet سپید follows دیو.
segments.s109-g82: که introduces the content of the reply (here functioning like 'that' / introducing the quoted clause). از + روزگاران is a prepositional phrase ('from/of the ages' — 'because of the times/fate'); مشو is the negative present/imperative of شدن ('do not become'); ناامید is the predicate adjective 'hopeless' addressed to the addressee (subject 'you' is omitted as normal in Persian imperatives).
segments.s109-g83: بیایم is a first‑person present/subjunctive form used here to state the speaker's immediate intention ('I will come' / 'I come'); کنون is an adverb 'now' placed after the verb for emphasis; با introduces the following accompaniment; سپاهی گران is a noun + postpositive adjective ('a heavy/mighty army' — گران used figuratively as 'mighty' or 'weighty' in poetic register).
segments.s109-g84: ببرم is the first‑person present/subjunctive of بردن ('I will take/lead/capture'); پی before the pronoun او means 'after/in pursuit of him' (a prepositional/adverbial element); ز is the poetic form of 'از' ('from') and takes the place name مازندران as origin. Word order (verb then adverbial phrases) is common in classical Persian poetic lines.
segments.s109-g85: This begins with a simple past clause شب آمد ('night came' = 'night fell'). یکی ابر is an indefinite numeral + noun ('a certain/a single cloud' or simply 'a cloud') and شد here functions like 'appeared'/'became' (شد used intransitively to mark coming into being). با سپاه is an accompanying prepositional phrase ('with the army/host').
segments.s109-g86: جهان کرد is object + past verb 'made/caused the world' (کرد as causative/making verb). چون introduces a simile 'like/as', followed by روی زنگی سیاه ('the face of a Zangi who is black/dark‑skinned') where زنگی is the noun and سیاه the postpositive adjective. The clause means the world was made to resemble the dark face (i.e., turned black/gloomy).
segments.s109-g87: چو is a temporal conjunction here ('when'). دریای قارست is a genitive/construct phrase ('the sea of Qarast' — دریای + following name/word قارست), and گفتی is the second‑person past of گفتن ('you said/called'); together the clause reads 'when you called (it) the sea of Qarast.' Note that classical Persian often omits an explicit pronoun for the object already understood from context.
segments.s109-g88: همه is a universal quantifier 'all'; روشناییش is روشنایی + possessive -ش ('its brightness'); گشته is the perfect/resultant participle or verbal adjective from گشتن used like 'has become'/'has turned'; نهان is the predicate adjective 'hidden.' The clause states that the world's entire brightness has become concealed.
segments.s109-g65: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when' introducing a temporal clause. یک هفته is the time phrase 'one week', and بگذشت is the past of گذشتن ('had passed'); ایرانیان is the plural subject that follows the temporal clause (classical word order: temporal clause first, subject/main predicate after).
segments.s109-g66: ز غارت is a prepositional/ablative phrase relating to the action (here: in/with respect to plundering); غارت is the noun 'plunder'. گشادند is the 3rd‑person plural past of گشادن used here intransitively as 'opened/widened' → 'spread/scattered'. یکسر میان is an adverbial phrase 'completely / all through the midst' modifying the verb (i.e. they spread everywhere to plunder).
segments.s109-g67: خبر شد is an impersonal/patient construction meaning 'news came / it became known'; شد here has the sense 'happen/come about'. سوی + noun marks the destination 'to the king'; شاه مازندران is the full title 'the king of Mazandaran.'
segments.s109-g68: دلش = دل + enclitic ش 'his' (possessive). گشت is past of گشتن 'became/turned'; here 'دلش گشت پر درد' = 'his heart became full of pain' where پر + noun means 'full of (something)'. و links the two clauses; سر شد گران is a parallel clause 'his head became heavy' (an idiom for grief/distress).
segments.s109-g69: ز دیوان here introduces the location/scene 'at the diwan' (diwan = the court/administrative hall). به پیش اندرون = 'before/in front of the inner (chamber)' (پیش used in the sense 'in the presence of / before'). سنجه بود is a simple existential clause 'there was a scale / measuring instrument' (سنجه = weighing/measuring device).
segments.s109-g70: که introduces an explanatory/cause clause 'for / because'; جان و تنش = 'his life and body' (جان and تن linked by و, with the possessive ش on تن referring back to the same possessor). زان سخن = 'by/from that speech/news' (زان = 'from that'); رنجه بود is the past passive-ish construction 'was distressed / afflicted.'
segments.s109-g71: بدو is the prepositional phrase 'to him'. گفت is the past verb 'he said' (the printed senses list here does not include 'say', so the exact sense mapping below is adjusted). رو in this position is the imperative 'go' (poetic/archaic imperative of رفتن, often written رو). نزد + noun means 'to the presence of / to (the person)'; دیو سپید = 'the white demon'.
segments.s109-g72: چنان = 'such' (qualifier). رو here is a noun 'appearance/face' (so not the imperative); the construction چنان رو که introduces a clause of manner 'put on such a look that...'. که introduces the result/aim 'so that'; بر چرخ گردنده = 'on/upon the revolving wheel' (چرخ = wheel/cycle; گردنده = rotating). شید here is an archaic/poetic form (contextually a subjunctive/optative 'you may become / you may be'); its dictionary senses given are not literal matches, so its precise morphological role is best read as a poetic subjunctive of شدن.

sentences.s109-s45: A certain man pitched a tent over him; smoke and tar blackened the world, and eyes were left staring — nothing good.
sentences.s109-s46: When the night passed and the day drew near, the world-seeker's sight grew dim.
sentences.s109-s47: From the host two portions were affected and gloom fell on the eyes; the heads of the famed were full of anger at him.
sentences.s109-s48: He did great harm to them as well; there had never been a thing like that misfortune.
sentences.s109-s69: When Arzhang heard his words, he turned his face toward the king of Mazandaran.
sentences.s109-s70: He continued on with his army, accompanied by the captives and the finely adorned horses.
sentences.s109-s71: He handed him over to the king and, light of heart, returned; he came to that lofty mountain from the broad plain.
sentences.s109-s17: Whomever you see, whether old or young, strike him down so that his spirit is no longer with him.
sentences.s109-s18: And burn every inhabited place you see of it; bring night upon some place where you will be by day.
sentences.s109-s19: Do this until news reaches the divs; make the world entirely empty of the divs.
sentences.s109-s20: Giv girded his loins and departed from the king's presence; from the army he chose and formed the troop Niv.
sentences.s109-s49: When King Kavus's sight grew dim, misfortune befell the army because of his conduct.
sentences.s109-s50: All the treasure was plundered and the army taken captive; youth, power, and fortune returned old.
sentences.s109-s51: One should learn all the tales, for they dazzle and make the onlooker stare in wonder upon wonder.
sentences.s109-s52: The spahbed said thus: when he saw the hardship, 'a vigilant command is better than treasure.'
sentences.s109-s29: They carried word of it to Kavus — news about the delight of that place and that nobility.
sentences.s109-s30: They kept saying 'how very delightful!' — for he had said that Mazandaran is a counterpart (a twin) of Paradise.
sentences.s109-s31: The whole city, it seems, is like an idol‑temple.
sentences.s109-s32: The idols are paradises, it seems; truly, Paradise washed its face upon their pomegranate‑blossoms.
sentences.s109-s53: He endured great hardship for about a week; he did not see any of the Iranians with his eyes.
sentences.s109-s54: A white demon roared in my paradise, saying, “O king without offspring, by your conduct you are like a willow.”
sentences.s109-s55: You ever kept your superiority in readiness; you sought the pasture of Mazandaran.
sentences.s109-s56: You displayed your strength like a drunken elephant, and you lent no one your hand.
sentences.s109-s1: When Zāl the spahbed left the flank, repeatedly he set the host’s face (i.e., put the army in motion) and moved forward.
sentences.s109-s2: The king ordered Tous and Goudarz to marshal the host and set its head on the road.
sentences.s109-s3: When night became day, the king and the warriors set their heads toward Mazandaran.
sentences.s109-s4: He entrusted the land of Iran to Milad: the key of the treasure, and the crown and the gem.
sentences.s109-s13: They spent the whole night decking the assembly with wine; at the turn of the night they rose from sleep.
sentences.s109-s14: They came up to the king in scattered groups, girded at the waist and wearing helmets.
sentences.s109-s15: Then he ordered that Giv be made shahriar, and again to choose a thousand from the army.
sentences.s109-s16: If anyone should turn to the heavy mace — the opener of the city of Mazandaran...
sentences.s109-s21: It came to pass that in the city of Mazandaran swords and heavy maces rained down.
sentences.s109-s22: Woman and child and man, steady (or in order), none found safety from the point of his sword — beware!
sentences.s109-s23: He was plundering and the city was burning; he put poison in the place of opium.
sentences.s109-s24: Once, when he saw the city like the highest heaven, he saw its gate full of delight.
sentences.s109-s61: They made every fetter for the chiefs when they were done with binding and fastening.
sentences.s109-s62: He gave them a little food to keep life in them, so that they might live day by day.
sentences.s109-s63: After that, all the treasure of the king of the world—whether a ruby crown or a heavy mace—
sentences.s109-s64: he entrusted whatever he saw from edge to edge to Arzhang, the commander of Mazandaran.
sentences.s109-s9: He fashioned a spot where the sun could hide, and made that place calm and fit for sleep.
sentences.s109-s10: Where the stronghold of the fierce divs was — the elephant feared that place.
sentences.s109-s11: He spread a gold-brocade over the ewes; the air, full of the scent of wine, was pleasantly fragrant.
sentences.s109-s12: All the champions, fortunate and in attendance, sat upon King Kavus's throne.
sentences.s109-s65: He turned toward the king and said, "Tell him: do not now seek an excuse from Ahriman."
sentences.s109-s66: All the champions of Iran and the king see neither the sun as bright nor the moon.
sentences.s109-s67: I did not kill (him); go and rebuke him loudly, and tell him so that he may know the rises and falls.
sentences.s109-s68: By lamentation and hardship his awareness returns, yet no one puts an ear to this matter.
sentences.s109-s25: On every village-square there were over a thousand worshippers, with necklaces and with earrings.
sentences.s109-s26: There were still more worshippers, wearing hats, their faces like the shining moon.
sentences.s109-s27: In every place treasures were scattered — gold; in one place a red dinar and a jewel.
sentences.s109-s28: Countless four-footed beasts were gathered around him; it was paradisal — you might call that place 'Hamīdūn'.
sentences.s109-s37: Tell him that a powerful army has come from Iran to Mazandaran for plunder.
sentences.s109-s38: Kavus the world-seeker went at their head — a newly formed army of war-makers.
sentences.s109-s39: If you are not now a rescuer, you will see that no living person remains in Mazandaran.
sentences.s109-s40: When he heard the message, Sanjeh hid (himself/it); he then told the demon the king’s message.
sentences.s109-s5: He said to him, "If the enemy should appear," — "You must draw the sword of vengeance."
sentences.s109-s6: From every ill take refuge with Zal and with Rostam, for they are the army's support and the ornament of the age.
sentences.s109-s7: Another day the sound of the war-drum arose; Goudarz and Tous kept driving the army.
sentences.s109-s8: Kavus kept going with the glorious army; he made camp (or struck a place) in front of Mount Esporoz.
sentences.s109-s57: When you sat with crown and throne, you thus deceived wisdom.
sentences.s109-s58: Now, that which is within your sphere of action is yours; your heart obtained those wishes which you sought.
sentences.s109-s59: From those manly divs he selected dagger‑bearers — two thousand and ten warriors.
sentences.s109-s60: He set a guardian over the Iranians, and filled the leaders' heads with care.
sentences.s109-s41: Thus the White Demon answered, "Do not despair because of the ages."
sentences.s109-s42: I will come now with a mighty host and take him from Mazandaran.
sentences.s109-s43: Night fell; a cloud appeared with the host and made the world like the face of a black Zangi.
sentences.s109-s44: When you called the world the sea of Qarast, all its brightness became hidden.
sentences.s109-s33: When a week had passed, the Iranians spread all about, intent on plunder.
sentences.s109-s34: The news reached the king of Mazandaran; his heart became full of pain and his head grew heavy.
sentences.s109-s35: In the diwan, before the inner chamber, there stood a scale, for his life and body were distressed by that news.
sentences.s109-s36: He said to him, 'Go to the white demon; take on such an appearance that you may be upon the revolving wheel.'
